coredata.dc:title: Two birds with one stone: Visual colorful assessment of dipicolinic acid and Cu2+ by Ln-MOF hybrid attapulgite nano-probe
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Xiangzhen
Xu, Jun
Li, Yongxin
Zhao, Tongqian
Zhang, Lina
Bi, Ning
Gou, Jian
Jia, Lei
coredata.dc:description: The poor solubility of Ln-MOF fluorescent nano-probe in water significantly limited their practical application. At the same time, the determination of target analytes is mostly carried out in buffer solution, but the preparation of buffer solution and pH calibration often bring inconvenience to the test. In this work, a bifunctional fluorescent nano-probe (ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF) was constructed at room temperature through one-step method using inorganic nanoclays ATP and Ln-MOF, which recognized anthrax biomarker DPA and Cu2+ in commercially pure drinking water. This nano-probe achieved visual detection of DPA with ultra-high sensitivity (LOD-DPA = 8.82 nM), excellent selectivity to DPA isomers and high reliability in the detection of DPA released by Bacillus subtilis. In addition, the probe can also selectively detect Cu2+ through the “turn-off” pathway with a high sensitivity (LOD-Cu2+ = 6.94 nM). Intelligent visual monitoring was realized by fast reading and analyzing the RGB values of the color changing images of the paper-based nano-probe using smartphone color scanning APP. Furthermore, thin-film-based luminescent nanoprobe can also make excellent visual color assessment of DPA and Cu2+, and advanced logic gate circuit analytical devices were also designed.

head.ce:title: Two birds with one stone: Visual colorful assessment of dipicolinic acid and Cu2+ by Ln-MOF hybrid attapulgite nano-probe

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The poor solubility of Ln-MOF fluorescent nano-probe in water significantly limited their practical application. At the same time, the determination of target analytes is mostly carried out in buffer solution, but the preparation of buffer solution and pH calibration often bring inconvenience to the test. In this work, a bifunctional fluorescent nano-probe (ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF) was constructed at room temperature through one-step method using inorganic nanoclays ATP and Ln-MOF, which recognized anthrax biomarker DPA and Cu2+ in commercially pure drinking water. This nano-probe achieved visual detection of DPA with ultra-high sensitivity (LOD-DPA = 8.82 nM), excellent selectivity to DPA isomers and high reliability in the detection of DPA released by Bacillus subtilis. In addition, the probe can also selectively detect Cu2+ through the “turn-off” pathway with a high sensitivity (LOD-Cu2+ = 6.94 nM). Intelligent visual monitoring was realized by fast reading and analyzing the RGB values of the color changing images of the paper-based nano-probe using smartphone color scanning APP. Furthermore, thin-film-based luminescent nanoprobe can also make excellent visual color assessment of DPA and Cu2+, and advanced logic gate circuit analytical devices were also designed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacillus anthracis is a kind of spore-type harmful bacteria, whose spores can resist various harsh environments, such as extremely high or low temperature, high pressure, strong acid and alkali and ultraviolet radiation, and can germinate as long as the surrounding environment is suitable [1]. More importantly, the spores of Bacillus anthracis can not only cause food poisoning and malignant diseases in meat or other daily products through soil and plants [2], but also cause the spread of bioterrorism as a potential biological warfare agent [3,4]. 2,6-dipicolinic acid (DPA) is the unique component of anthrax spores, which accounts for 5–15 % of dry weight and it can be used as a special biomarker of anthrax [5]. In addition, copper ion (Cu2+) is an important environmental pollutant, and the imbalance of Cu2+ level in organisms can cause stress response, homeostasis disorders [6–8] and serious neurodegenerative diseases [9]. Therefore, it is of great significance in the field of biology and environment to develop new dual-functional fluorescent MOFs materials for rapid and efficient trace detection of DPA and Cu2+. Recently, the quantitative analysis of DPA favored by researchers include surface enhanced Raman method [10,11], electrochemical method [12] and ratio fluorescence method [1,2,13,14]. Among these detection technologies, the ratio fluorescence technology usually contains a self-calibration system, which can solve the false negative or false positive results caused by the interference of irrelevant factors such as environment and instrument caused by a single fluorescence detection [15,16]. Simultaneously, it is more conducive to point-of-care testing (POCT) detection of DPA by fluorescent paper based detection method with obvious fluorescence color changes [17].

Fluorescent sensing metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) functional materials play an indispensable role in the field of environment and biology. However, numerous MOFs materials were limited in water-based applications due to their poor water solubility. Moreover, multifunctional fluorescent probes that can simultaneously detect multiple targets are also rare. In the construction of ratiometric fluorescence probes, the superior framework structure of MOFs was often used to embed the chromophore as reference signal [18,19]. Whereas, the fluorescent probes constructed by embedding in the MOF cavity may have a problem of reducing fluorescence intensity due to leakage of the embedded chromophore. Therefore, it is urgent to develop hybrid materials directly using MOFs as recognition signals [20]. In view of the remarkable affinity of DPA for rare earth ions (especially Eu(III) and Tb(III)), and the advantages of Ln-DPA complexes such as high luminous intensity, narrow emission peak, large stokes shift and long luminescence lifetime, Ln-based nanomaterials had been widely used in DPA detection [5,21,22]. The lanthanide metal–organic frameworks (Ln-MOFs) formed by introducing lanthanide elements with unique luminescent properties into MOFs had good fluorescence performance [23–27]. Subsequently, the introduction of suitable organic ligand in Ln-MOFs can greatly enhance their luminous brightness by “antenna effect” [28]. These luminescent Ln-MOFs can be used not only as fluorescent probes [29], but also as energy transfer platforms to construct host–guest systems [30,31]. Although most of the Ln-MOFs-based probes with one single lanthanide element had good fluorescence properties, there is still a disadvantage of chromophore leakage in the construction of ratio fluorescence probes.

Multiple lanthanide elements can be introduced concurrently to acquire MOF materials with different emission centers in different emission bands. Furthermore, the luminescence color can be manipulated and the sensing performance can be upgraded by controlling or changing the proportion of lanthanide elements in Ln-MOFs [32]. At present, the application of these Ln-MOFs with double rare earth elements in fluorescence probes have attracted the attention of many research groups [1,33–35]. However, these Ln-MOFs-type proportional fluorescence probes with fluorescence color-tunable and self-calibration system also have inherent defects in the detection of water-soluble targets. Consequently, the introduction or encapsulation of some inorganic clay matrices with good water solubility into the framework of MOFs can improve their water solubility and the detection sensitivity.

Attapulgite (ATP), as a representative of inorganic natural clay, has the advantages of porous structure, high specific surface area, adsorption, cation exchange, water dispersibility and thermal stability [36], which becomes a potential candidate for the preparation of various nanocomposites [37,38]. In addition, compared with other nano-substrate carriers, ATP is also abundant reserves, inexpensive, and easy availability [39,40]. Meanwhile, the introduction of ATP into Ln-MOFs may improve their water dispersion stability, providing a new method for improving the water solubility of MOFs.

In this work, ATP was introduced into Ln-MOFs to construct a bifunctional responsive fluorescent nano-probe (ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF) by one-step method, which realized the rapid visual detection of DPA and Cu2+ in commercially pure drinking water. The synthesis method of ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF and a dual-target detection strategy for DPA and Cu2+ are illustrated in Scheme 1 . The organic linker involved in the coordination in the preparation process can easily transfer energy to Eu3+, resulting bright red fluorescence of the probe. At the same time, the introduction of ATP also made the probe had excellent water dispersion stability and fluorescence enhancement characteristics. In the aqueous detection of DPA, noticeable visual luminescence color changes and excellent selectivity to DPA isomers can be produced, which was due to the fact that DPA can easily cooperate with Tb3+ and sensitize Tb3+ to induce the transfer of luminescent centers in MOF. The practicability of the nano-probe was also verified, and the LOD for Bacillus subtilis spores was 1.04 × 105 spores/mL. Studies also showed that the nano-probe can also perform ultra-sensitive selective detection of Cu2+ by “turn-off” fluorescence, and the presence of Cu2+ induced the fluorescence color of the nano-probe to decrease from red to colorless. The RGB value of the colorful images of the paper-based probe was quickly read and analyzed by smartphone color scanning APP to realize visual fluorescent sensing. The fluorescence color change of the luminescent thin-film sensor was consistent with that of the paper-based probe, and the logic gate circuits for DPA and Cu2+ fluorescence detection were constructed. The construction strategy of this novel bifunctional nano-probe not only effectively upgraded the fluorescence stability and detection sensitivity of DPA and Cu2+, but also facilitated the rapid, accurate and visual detection of other biomarkers, which opened up a new avenue for the development of lanthanide luminescent nano-probe.

Attapulgite and all the initial chemical reagents used in the experiment were purchased from commercial resources without further purification. The aqueous solutions in the experiment were prepared with ultrapure water. The commercially pure drinking Yibao water was obtained from the local supermarket. Microstructures of various materials were observed by Hitachi S-3000 N scanning electron microscope (SEM) at 5 kV accelerated voltage. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), and EDX mapping were performed using TecnaiG2 F30 at 300 kV accelerated voltage. The powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) pattern in the 2θ range of 5° to 80° was obtained at the speed of 10°/min at 40 kV voltage and 150 mA current using the RigakuDmax2400 diffractometer radiated by Cu Kα. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were measured in the wavenumber range of 4000–400 cm−1 using BrukerV70 infrared spectrometer and KBr tableting technology. The surface composition of the samples was analyzed by Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, 1486.6 eV, 6 mA × 12 kV) with monochromatic Al Kα source. After the sample was digested with nitric acid, the contents of Eu and Tb were determined by an inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometer (ICP-AES, Aglient 7800, pump speed 29 r/min, nebulizer flow rate 0.86 L/min, auxiliary gas 0.7 L/min, sample washing time 40 s, RF power 1300 W). The steady-state luminescence spectra were measured by F97Pro fluorescence spectrometer. The excitation wavelength was 275 nm, the scanning speed was 1000 nm/min, and the slit width was 10 nm. The fluorescence lifetime decay spectra of emission peaks at 545 and 617 nm were studied by using Edinburgh FS5 full-steady-state fluorescence spectrometer with a frozen R928P photomultiplier tube.

ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF nanomaterials were prepared through an improved method based on the previous literature [41,42]. Specifically, 100 mg of ATP was evenly dissolved in 5 mL of ultrapure water, followed by the addition of 3 mL aqueous solution of Eu(NO3)3·6H2O (0.01 mmol), Tb(NO3)3·6H2O (0.06 mmol), and sodium acetate (NaAC, 0.2 mmol). Then, 2,4,6-tris(4-carboxyphenyl)-1,3,5-triazine (H3TATB, 0.07 mmol) in 20 mL methanol solution was added into the above mixed solution and stirred violently at room temperature for 30 min. The white precipitate was obtained under centrifugation at 8000 r/min and washed three times with methanol and water (v/v = 1:1). The solid powder dried at 60 °C and emitted red fluorescence under 254 nm UV lamp.

50 μL of ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF nano-probe aqueous solution (1 mg/mL) was taken into commercially pure drinking water. The different concentrations of DPA were added (0–20 μM) and adjusted the final volume of the mixture to 2 mL. The fluorescence spectra and the corresponding fluorescence photos were recorded at 275 nm excitation and 254 nm UV lamp, respectively. The detection process of Cu2+ was similar to that of DPA, the final concentration of Cu2+ was between 0 and 10 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DPA in bacterial spores
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the safety of the experiment, the non-infectious Bacillus subtilis was used to replace Bacillus anthracis for practicability study. Firstly, Bacillus subtilis was cultured at 37 °C for 3–4 days to induce spore formation [43,44]. The spores were collected by centrifugation at low temperature (4 °C) and washed with distilled water for 5 times to remove the medium. A certain number of the washed samples were dispersed in dodecylamine solution (1 mM), and then heated at 90 °C for half an hour to promote the germination process and release DPA. The concentration of spores was measured by cytometry. The storage solution of spores was prepared into 3.6 × 108 spores/mL aqueous solution. The high concentration spore solution was diluted into different concentrations immediately, and the concentration range was from 3.6 × 106 spores/mL to 18 × 106 spores/mL. The characteristic emission peak intensity of ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF (50 μL, 1 mg/mL) was recorded in the presence of different concentrations of spores.

Successful immobilization of ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF nano-probe on test paper was an indispensable procedure for visual detection. Firstly, the ordinary filter paper was cut into small discs with a diameter of about 7 mm, and then impregnated in the aqueous dispersion of ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF nano-probe (1 mg/mL) for 30 min. The immobilized test paper loaded with ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF was obtained after natural drying. Various concentrations of DPA or Cu2+ solutions were dripped on the prepared test paper and the fluorescence color changes of the test paper under the irradiation of 254 nm UV lamp were recorded and the RGB analysis was achieved by a smartphone application. Considering the influence of environment on the stability of test paper, the test was carried out at room temperature of 25 °C and relative humidity of 50 %.

Since hydrophilic film and hydrophobic film cannot meet the test requirements, semi-hydrophobic luminescent film was the first choice for photo collections of targets sensing in aqueous solution. SA/PVA/ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF nano-probe membrane was fabricated according to the previous literature [45,46]. Specifically, 2 % sodium alginate (SA, 28 mL) solution was mixed with 5 % polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, 5 mL) solution, followed by the addition of 1.0 g glycerol as a plasticizer. The mixture was stirred intensely at room temperature for 1 h, followed by the addition of 2 mL of aqueous solution containing 50 mg of ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF. The uniform water suspension obtained after continuous stirring for 1 h was poured onto the glass plate. The dried film was crosslinked with 0.1 % CaCl2 solution to enhance the toughness and hydrophobicity of the film. Then, the luminescent thin films can be obtained after the residual CaCl2 was removed with distilled water. In the response test, the fluorescence color of the films after adding various concentrations of DPA or Cu2+ under 254 nm UV light were recorded by smartphones.

The morphology of ATP and ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF were investigated by SEM and TEM. It can be seen from the SEM images that the edge of a single ATP was clear and uniformly dispersed, while the edge became blurred and easy to aggregate after being combined with Eu:Tb-MOF, which may be related to the interaction between ATP and Eu:Tb-MOF (Fig. S1). ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF still remained the fiber rod-like structure of ATP, but the coarse surface contrasted with pure ATP (Fig. 1 a and b), which was caused by the one-pot modification of rare earth framework. The energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS, Fig. S2) indicated that besides Mg, Al, O, and Si elements from the ATP matrix, there were also Eu and Tb elements. The spatial distribution of Si, Al, O, Eu and Tb elements in ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF nanoprobe was verified by element mapping (Fig. S3) that the Eu:Tb-MOF layer was extensively modified on ATP nanorods.

The crystal structure of ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF nano-probe was further characterized by XRD (Fig. 1c). The characteristic lattice planes (110), (200), (130), (040), (121), (231), (400) and (161) in ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF matched well with the pure ATP (JCPDS 29–0855) [47,48], corresponding to the 8.57°, 13.92°, 16.51°, 19.98°, 21.03°, 26.83°, 27.66°, and 35.39° characteristic peaks. The main characteristic diffraction peak of Eu:Tb-MOF at 6.08° can be obviously observed in the spectrum of ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF, but the weak diffraction peaks at 7.37°, 8.83°, and 11.79° were not obvious due to the relatively small loading of Eu:Tb-MOF. Considering that the characteristic diffraction peak position of the prepared Eu:Tb-MOF was consistent with that of the simulated Tb-TATB, it was confirmed that the material preparation was successful. The FTIR spectra of ATP, Eu:Tb-MOF, and ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF are shown in Fig. 1d for comparison. The characteristic peaks at 1659, 1030, and 984 cm−1 corresponded to the vibration of Si-OH, Si-O-Si, and Si-O-Mg in the ATP spectrum [49,50]. The bonds at 774 and 832 cm−1 vibrations of the CH bond were due to the rigid benzene ring of the main organic ligand H3TATB in Eu:Tb-MOF. The bonds at 1403/1356 cm−1 and 1533 cm−1 were ascribed to the vibration of the CN bond in the N heterocyclic ring [51,52], and the symmetric stretching vibration of CN/OCO in the benzene ring [53], respectively.

The surface chemical composition of ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF nano-probe was further determined by the XPS spectrum. The binding energies of 284.81, 398.88, 532.34, 1133.22, and 1241.28 eV corresponded to C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, Eu 3d, and Tb 3d, respectively (Fig. 2 a). The binding energies of Tb 3d and Eu 3d were low due to the low loading of Eu:Tb-MOF thin layer. However, as can be seen in Fig. S4a, the peak can be divided into two bonds energies of Tb 3d5/2 (1241.5 eV) and Tb 3d3/2 (1277 eV) [54], respectively. In theory, the element content ratio of Eu to Tb was 1:6, which made the valence form of Eu 3d (Fig. S4b) cannot be clearly seen, while it can be found the successful loading of Eu element from the content percentage of each element in XPS scan data (Fig. S5). In addition, the red fluorescence emitted by the material also confirmed the existence of Eu element. Furthermore, the contents of Tb and Eu confirmed by ICP were 6.05 %, and 1.06 %, respectively, which were consistent with theoretical values. The C 1s spectra exhibited 284.5, 285.1, 286.0, and 288.2 eV, which belonged to CC, CC/CN, CO, and OCO/N CN bonds [55–57], respectively (Fig. 2b). The characteristic peaks at 398.9 eV and 402.8 eV in the N 1s spectra (Fig. 2c) were attributed to NC and NC bonds [58], respectively. The 530.5, 532.3, and 533.8 eV in the O 1s spectra (Fig. 2d) corresponded to the CO, CO and OH bonds from H3TATB and auxiliary ligand NaAC [59,60], respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescent properties and optimization of detection conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence performance was the primary standard for the performance evaluation of fluorescent nano-probe. The equivalent amount of ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF and Eu:Tb-MOF nano-sensor were prepared with the same concentration, and their water solubility and luminescent stability were investigated. Compared with the easy sedimentation of Eu:Tb-MOF, ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF exhibited better water solubility (Fig. S6a), which can still maintain good dispersibility and fluorescence stability even after standing for a long time (greater than30 min) (Fig. S6b). Meanwhile, the change of fluorescence lifetime of solid materials before and after ATP introduction was also investigated. As shown in Fig. S7, the presence of ATP had little effect on the fluorescence lifetime at the Tb emission position (545 nm), but can prolong the fluorescence lifetime of Eu (617 nm) from 0.902 ms to 1.031 ms (Table S1). The introduction of inorganic clay matrix can not only improve the water dispersion and fluorescence stability of rare earth MOF, but also delay its life decay rate. More importantly, it can reduce the amount of rare earth elements in the final probe, which was expected to improve the detection performance and reduce the detection cost.

Firstly, the fluorescence property of the synthesized ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF nano-probe was discussed. ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF containing two rare earth metals simultaneously emitted the characteristic emission peaks of Tb3+ and Eu3+ with the excitation of 275 nm. The five bands at about 490, 545, 592, 617 and 694 nm were assigned to the 5D4-7FJ (J = 6, 5) transition of Tb3+ and the 5D0-7FJ (J = 1, 2, 4) transition of Eu3+, respectively [42]. Secondly, the excitation stability, the different medium and acid-base stability, and the response to DPA at different pH of ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF were investigated to optimize the detection conditions. Fig. S8a indicated that the fluorescence ratio (I545/I617) of ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF had little difference in the excitation wavelength range of 260–290 nm, so the excitation wavelength of 275 nm with relatively high fluorescence ratio was selected as the optimal excitation wavelength. In addition, the stability of ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF in different medium solutions (such as NaCl, methanol, ethanol, DMF and DMSO) was also evaluated. ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF exhibited bright red fluorescence in the above media and the fluorescence intensity ratio fluctuated little within 10 min, indicating that ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF had good optical stability in these mediums (Fig. S8b). As shown in Fig. S9a, the fluorescence intensity of the nano-probe was relatively stable at different pH (Tris-HCl buffer, pH 5–8) and the fluorescence color was almost the same. However, with the increase of the basicity of the buffer system (pH > 8), the nano-probe will aggregate and lead to the decrease of fluorescence intensity, so the optimal working pH of the nano-probe was not greater than 8. The fluorescence stability within 10 min under high-pressure xenon lamp irradiation was shown in Fig. S9b, indicating that ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF had excellent optical stability. With the introduction of DPA, the nano-probe showed a good response at pH 8 (Fig. S10a), and the optimal response time was 2 min (Fig. S10b). Due to the low degree of color visualization (Fig. 3 a) of the fluorescence photos of the nano-probe responsed to DPA under the optimal conditions of pH 8, and the difference of fluorescence color in pH 7–8 was not very obvious, we tried to obtain the fluorescence photos in Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7) and commercially pure drinking water. From the comparison of fluorescence images in Fig. 3b and Fig. 3c, the evolution of fluorescence color from red to green was distinct. To facilitate the detection process, commercially pure drinking water was selected as the best solvent for the response test, and the operation of buffer preparation was also omitted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response mechanism of luminescent nano-probe to DPA
ce:section.ce:para: Given the sensitive response of the nano-probe to DPA, the specific response mechanism was explored (Fig. 4 a). The △E values between T1 of DPA (25510 cm−1) and the resonant energy levels of Tb3+ (5D4, 20500 cm−1) and Eu3+ (5D0, 17200 cm−1) were 5010 cm−1 and 8310 cm−1 [41], respectively, indicating that the energy of DPA ligand can be better transferred to Tb3+ rather than Eu3+. Therefore, with the addition of DPA, the fluorescence intensity at 617 and 545 nm of the detection system gradually decreased and increased, respectively, realizing the transfer of the luminescent center from Eu3+ to Tb3+, which resulted in the gradual evolution of the fluorescence color from red to green (Fig. 4b and c). The energy transfer from Tb to Eu in MOFs was interrupted by the presence of DPA, which can be explained by the following reasons: (i) Although DPA can also coordinate with Eu3+, the lowest triplet energy level of DPA was more suitable for sensitizing Tb3+, which due to the Tb:Eu atomic ratio was 5.71 in the actual sample and the coordination probability of Eu3+ was less than Tb3+. (ii) The competition between DPA and organic ligand (H3TATB) in absorbing excitation energy may reduce the “antenna effect” of H3TATB on lanthanide ions, thereby affecting the energy transfer from Tb-to-Eu. (iii) The energy transfer of Tb-to-Eu was mainly controlled by the phonon-assisted Fӧrster transfer mechanism, and the coordination of DPA-Tb may affect the phonon vibration in Eu:Tb-MOF lattice, and ultimately affected the energy transfer of Tb-to-Eu [42,61,62]. Fig. 4c showed that the luminescence intensity of Eu3+ decreases while Tb3+ was sensitized by DPA ligand. This phenomenon can be explained by the formation of electron transfer bridge between DPA and Eu3+/Tb3+, and the competition with H3TATB ligand to sensitized Eu3+ or Tb3+. The high energy difference between H3TATB and Eu3+ or Tb3+ indicated that H3TATB was more inclined to sensitize Eu3+, while DPA can more effectively sensitize Tb3+. Therefore, the introduction of DPA will replace H3TATB to sensitize Tb3+, which greatly increased the peak intensity of I545, but to some extent hindered the energy transfer from H3TATB to Eu3+, resulting in the decrease of I617. However, due to the energy transfer from H3TATB to Eu3+ and from Tb3+ to Eu3+, IEu did not completely disappear. The mechanism of this competition between DPA and ligands to increase ITb and decrease IEu was also described in the previous literature on the detection of DPA by Eu/Tb bimetallic MOF or complex [34,63,64].

To further explore the interaction between DPA and ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF, the fluorescence lifetime of ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF before and after adding DPA were investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 4d and 4e, the lifetime of Tb3+ and Eu3+ increased with the increase of DPA, but the lifetime decay time of Tb3+ increased more obviously. When the DPA concentration reached 20 μM, the fluorescence lifetimes of Tb3+ and Eu3+ were 1.262 ms and 1.172 ms, respectively, which were significantly longer than those of Tb3+ and Eu3+ in ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF at 0.185 ms and 0.652 ms, respectively. This phenomenon was due to that DPA can transfer the absorbed energy to the rare earth ions through the “antenna effect”, and replace the water molecules in the coordination sphere, thus inhibited the non-radiative quenching effect and enhance the characteristic fluorescence of Tb3+ [14]. The efficiency of energy transfer ( η ) from DPA to Tb3+ and Eu3+ was calculated according to the following equation (1) and (2) [65]:

were 85.34 % and 44.37 %, separately, which further confirmed that the effective energy transfer between DPA and rare-earth ions in ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF was more conducive to the sensitization of Tb3+. The corresponding photophysical data were listed in Table S2.

Sensitivity is a critical indicator to evaluate the superior performance of the nano-probe. The fluorescence titration was used to further determine the sensitivity of ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF to DPA. With the increase of DPA from 0 to 20 μM, the emission intensity of Tb3+ enhanced significantly, while the emission intensity of Eu3+ decreased remarkably so that the sensing system showed a visible fluorescence color change from red to green (Fig. 5 a). In addition, the best linear relationship between the intensity ratio of I545/I617 and DPA concentrations was determined by the average of three measurements. It can be seen from Fig. 5b that the I545/I617 ratio exhibited a good linear relationship in the range of 0.1–2 μM and 2–20 μM of DPA, and the linear correlation equations were as follows:

The minimum limit of detection (LOD) of ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF for DPA was 8.82 nM according to 3σ/K (σ is the standard deviation of the blank sample measurement, and K is the slope of the calibration equation (3)), which was higher than the sensitivity of other fluorescent probes (Table 1 ), and far lower than the infection dose of anthracnose spores on the human body (60 μM) [18,66]. The limit of quantification (LOQ) based on 10 σ/K was 29.4 nM, which met the needs of sensitive detection in an aqueous solution and had great application potential. Meanwhile, the response fluorescence spectra of Eu:Tb-MOF without the employ of ATP to various concentrations of DPA (Fig. S11a and Fig. S11b) were also tested to further prove the superior function of ATP. The LOD and LOQ of Eu:Tb-MOF for DPA were 8.96 nM and 29.87 nM, respectively, and the change of fluorescence color (Fig. S11c) was not obvious, which was detrimental to visual detection of naked eyes.

According to the international chromaticity coordinate (CIE) map calculated by fluorescence spectral integration (Fig. 5c, Table S3), it can be clearly seen that with the increase of DPA content, the fluorescence color of ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF nano-probe presented a slow transition from red to green, which was consistent with the fluorescence photographs of colorimetric dish taken by smartphones under 254 nm UV light (Fig. 5d). This indicated that the prepared nano-probe showed explicit fluorescence variation and visualization for DPA sensing, which also established a foundation for the preparation of paper-based sensor and luminescent thin-filmed sensor.

Selectivity is another major indicator for evaluating the performance of probes. Anti-interference test and specific isomers detection were carried out to sufficiently prove the ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF nano-probe had better detection performance. In the anti-interference test, the effects of common carboxyl-containing small molecules and metal cations on the fluorescence properties of nano-probe were studied, such as benzoic acid (BA), pyridinecarboxylic acid (PA), nicotinic acid (NA), terephthalic acid (p-PA), isophthalic acid (m-PA), isonicotinic acid (INA) and 1,3,5-trimellitic acid (HTA), amino acids such as lysine (Lys), cysteine (Cys), glutathione (GSH), glycine (Gly) and l-aspartic acid (Asp), K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+ and Fe3+. As indicated in Fig. 6 a, the results showed that the influence of the above potential interfering substances on the nano-probe could be ignored except for Cu2+. Whereas, the presence of Cu2+ can induce the red fluorescence of the nano-probe to be gradually quenched, which was different from the change of the fluorescence color to DPA (Fig. 6b), indicating that the nano-probe had specific selectivity to DPA and Cu2+. The specific detection results of DPA isomers showed that the ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF nano-probe only had a strong effect on the carboxyl groups at 2- and 6- positions on the pyridine ring, but had no significant response to the DPA isomers as 2,3-, 2,4-, 3,4- and 3,5-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (Fig. 6c and d). This confirmed that the specific selectivity of the nano-probe to DPA was associated with its molecular configuration. The high selectivity of ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF to DPA can be attributed to the strong interaction between the nitrogen atom on the pyridine ring and the oxygen atom in the two carboxylic acid groups at specific positions of the DPA molecule with Eu3+ and Tb3+. Although the above analogues also had oxygen or nitrogen atoms, their coordination ability to rare-earth ions was weaker than that of DPA and cannot completely replace all coordination water molecules. In addition, the lowest triplet energy level of DPA was more suitable for transferring energy to Tb3+ than other aromatic carboxylic acids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DPA in actual bacterial spores
ce:section.ce:para: Based on proving that ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF nano-probe has good sensitivity and selectivity to DPA, the detection of DPA released by Bacillus subtilis was further studied. As shown in Fig. 7 , with the increase of the germinated spore suspension, the green fluorescence of ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF at 545 nm gradually increased and the red fluorescence at 617 nm decreased, eventually leading to the transfer of luminescent centers. According to the linear relationship between I545/I617 and concentrations of spores, the LOD was calculated to be 1.04 × 105 spore/mL, which indicated that the nano-probe had strong practicability for DPA released by germinated spores and great potential in the application of DPA sensitive detection in actual environment.

The results of interference experiments showed that the introduction of Cu2+ would quench the fluorescence of the nano-probe. Similar to the DPA detection process, 2 mL of aqueous solution containing 50 μg nano-probe was taken as the detection system. As indicated in Fig. S12a, with the introduction of Cu2+, the red fluorescence was gradually quenched. At the same time, the response of the nano-probe to Cu2+ was very rapid, and the optimal response state can be achieved in only 40 s (Fig. S12b). The response of the material to Cu2+ was studied by fluorescence titration method. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the fluorescence intensity of the nano-probe at 545 nm and 617 nm decreased gradually with the increase of Cu2+ concentrations, especially the ultrasensitive transition intensity at 617 nm decreased rapidly, which made the red fluorescence of the detection system gradually quenched. Fig. 8b indicated there was a good positive correlation between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I545/I617) and Cu2+ concentration (0–10 μM):
(5)

According to this linear relationship and the threefold signal-to-noise ratio, the detection limit of Cu2+ was calculated to be 6.94 nM, which was lower than the detection limit of Cu2+ in most previous literature (Table 2 ), especially higher than the sensitivity of most proportional fluorescent probes based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [79]. The fluorescence images of ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF nano-probe responding to various concentrations of Cu2+ were taken by smartphone upon a 254 nm UV light. As illustrated in Fig. 8c, the fluorescence color gradually changed from red to colorless, which may be attributed to two reasons: (i) the coagulation caused by the complexation of Cu2+ with ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF, (ii) the strong adsorption of Cu2+ by ATP itself and the MOF layer. At the same time, the existence of Cu2+ will reduce the fluorescence lifetime of ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF, as shown in Fig. S13 and Table S4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DPA and Cu2+ by paper-based nano-probe
ce:section.ce:para: Because the ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF nano-probe had the advantages of simple synthesis, high sensitivity, good selectivity, and obvious color change in the present of DPA and Cu2+, a portable paper-based DPA and Cu2+ detection device was developed. The ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF nano-probe was successfully immobilized on filter paper. Under the irradiation of 254 nm UV lamp, the fluorescence images of ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF nano-probe to various concentrations of DPA and Cu2+ were obtained by smartphone. The fluorescence color of the test papers gradually changed from red to green with the increase of DPA concentration (Fig. 9 a), while the fluorescence color of Cu2+ gradually changed from red to puce during the detection (Fig. 9c), and these changes can be observed by the naked eye.

Furthermore, visual semi-quantitative sensing analysis of DPA and Cu2+ were achieved by digitizing the red (R), green (G), and blue (B) color intensities in a fluorescent color image containing the sample system using color scanning application (App) in a portable smartphone as a signal reader and analyzer [48,80–82]. The correlation relation between the G/R ratio and the concentration of DPA (0–20 μM) and Cu2+ (0–10 μM) (Fig. 9b and d) were used as a reference, and the concentration range of DPA and Cu2+ could be calculated according to the fluorescence color. The portable detection device realized the convenient and rapid detection of the target, and made up for the defect that large instruments were not convenience for on-site inspection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DPA and Cu2+by luminescent thin-filmed sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Although the test paper can realize the rapid visual detection of DPA and Cu2+, the general filter paper will inevitably have background interference, and because the test paper is easy to be wetted by water, it will also affect the subsequent collection of colorful images. In order to improve the accuracy and operability of device-based detection, the semi-hydrophobic luminescent thin films SA/PVA/ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF were prepared, which showed bright red fluorescence under a 254 nm UV lamp. A series of fluorescence color changes were photographed after the addition of different concentrations of DPA or Cu2+ aqueous solution. Fig. 10 indicated that when DPA and Cu2+ aqueous solution contact with the film, visible fluorescence color changes can be observed even at a lower concentration. This was consistent with the color change trend of the paper-based detection device, but the discoloration was more sensitive. When the concentration of DPA was as low as 2 μM, the fluorescence color of the thin-film sensor had obviously changed from red to orange. And when the concentration was more than 4 μM, it gradually changed to green, while the paper-based detector cannot change until 5 μM and 10 μM. Meanwhile, the red quenching process of Cu2+ at the concentration of 1–3 μM can sensitively reflect the concentration change on paper-based sensors and thin-filmed sensors. The above results indicated that the satisfactory fluorescence performance of ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF can be used as a chemical nano-probe for real-time detection, and also provided a new idea for the design of rare earth hybrid luminescent sensing platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Logic gate circuit design for DPA and Cu2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the previous research foundation of intelligent and rapid analysis of the target by logic gate circuit [83,84], parallel logic gates consisting of two basic logic gate circuits (Fig. S14a and Fig. S15a) were constructed for semi-quantitative detection of DPA and Cu2+ concentrations, respectively. The fluorescence color was used as the input signal, and the DPA and Cu2+ concentrations were used as the output signals. The normalized luminescence intensity at the emission of 617 nm (IRed, input signal 1) and 545 nm (IGreen, input signal 2) were named as the double input signal (Fig. S14b and Fig. S15b). When the detection system exhibited red fluorescence or not, the input signals of input 1 were defined as “1″ and “0”, respectively. Similarly, the input letters for input 2 were defined as “1” and “0” when the detection system presents green fluorescence or not, respectively. Combined with the actual fluorescence detection, the doses thresholds of DPA were 2.5 μM and 8 μM, while the doses threshold of Cu2+ was 5 μM.

For the detection of DPA, when the concentration level of DPA was lower than 2.5 μM, the green fluorescence intensity in the detection system was much lower than the red fluorescence, defined as red fluorescence emitting only. When the concentration of DPA over 8 μM, the green fluorescence intensity in the detection system was much higher than the red fluorescence, so it is defined as green fluorescence emitting only. When the concentration of DPA is between 2.5 and 8 μM, it was defined as both green and red fluorescence emitting, resulting in a final orange fluorescence. Therefore, when the input signal was (1, 0) and the output signal was (1, 0, 0), indicating that the content of DPA in the detected system was at a low concentration level of less than 2.5 μM; when the input signal was (1, 1) and the output signal was (0, 1, 0), indicating that the content of DPA was at the medium concentration level of 2.5–8 μM; when the input signal was (0, 1) and the output signal was (0, 0, 1), indicating that the DPA content was at a high concentration level greater than 8 μM (Fig. S14c). For the detection of Cu2+, when the red fluorescence intensity in the detection system was dominant, the input (1,0) accompanied by output (1,0) indicated that the Cu2+ content was at a low concentration level of less than 5 μM; when the red fluorescence intensity dropped to almost the same as the green light intensity, the input (0, 0) accompanied by output (0, 1) indicated that the Cu2+ content was at a high concentration level greater than 5 μM (Fig. S15c).

In summary, the rare-earth fluorescent bifunctional nano-probe ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF with good water solubility was designed by a “two birds, one stone” strategy, which can recognize anthrax biomarker DPA and Cu2+ in commercially pure drinking water. The nano-probe can specifically respond to DPA (LOD = 8.82 nM), and the fluorescence color gradually changed from red to green with the increase of DPA but had no obvious response to the isomers of DPA. In addition, the practicability and reliability of the nano-probe were verified by the test of DPA released by spores of Bacillus subtilis. Meanwhile, the nano-probe could react with Cu2+ to quench the red fluorescence (LOD = 6.94 nM). Paper-based and luminescent thin-filmed sensor certified that ATP@Eu:Tb-MOF had excellent fluorescence performance and high visualization during the sensing process. The design of parallel logic gate circuit also provided the possibility in smart device applications. This kind of multi-target detection platform can not only meet the needs of sensitive detection in an aqueous solution, but also provide a new idea for the construction of a proportional fluorescence sensing platform.
